overall_summary: This competition focused on real-time financial market forecasting using non-stationary tabular time-series data, where traditional baselines quickly plateaued. Winning approaches universally pivoted to deep learning architectures, heavily leveraging online learning pipelines to adapt to distribution shifts during inference. Success hinged on meticulous data normalization, strategic feature engineering, and robust ensembling strategies tailored to strict latency constraints.
competition_flows: Raw tabular time-series data standardized/zero-imputed, augmented with rolling stats/market averages, fed into GRUs with auxiliary targets & weighted R² loss, optimized for inference speed, updated via single-pass online learning, then unweighted ensembled.
Raw data normalized with StandardScaler, NaNs filled with zeros, fed into 50/50 transformer encoder ensemble fine-tuned daily on recent 7 days with reduced LR.
Raw financial data organized into daily 3D tensors, augmented with cross-symbol means/z-scores/temporal diffs, fed into online-trained MLP with responder targets, ensembled, and submitted.

Raw data preprocessed with standardization/NaN-filling/periodic encodings, fed into TabM/AutoencoderMLP updated daily via online learning on rolling window, while offline LGBM/XGB/Ridge trained on fixed ranges, then ensembled.
Raw data loaded with 79 features, NaNs zero-filled, all responders as targets; models trained on sliding windows, validated on last 120 days, updated daily via online learning, then ensembled.

Raw financial data masked/normalized for ragged/non-stationary tensors, fed into neural network with stateful gates/attention, trained with online updates & zero-mean R² loss while mimicking API format, validated via temporal holdout.
Raw symbol features extracted and fed into 3-layer MLP to directly regress responders, with careful training/architectural tuning for generalization.
data_processing: Data filtering to start from date_id = 700
Organization into 3D tensors cut into one-day chunks
Computation of cross-symbol means and symbol-specific z-scores
Sin/cos periodic encodings for time_id and feature_61
Random sampling for online training window
Lagged responders' mean and standard deviation computation
Masking and careful normalization for ragged/unstable data
features_engineering: Rolling averages/standard deviations over last 1000 time_ids
Auxiliary responder targets (responder_9, responder_10) via shifted columns
cv_strategies: Time-series cross-validation with two folds (200 dates each) plus a 200-day gap holdout on the final 200 dates
Time-based holdout on the last 120 days with training windows of 978, 800, and 600 days tested
Temporal holdout of the last 120 days with strict temporal order and momentum-tracking metrics
ensembling: Simple unweighted average of predictions from multiple GRU architectures and seeds
50/50 ensemble of two transformer encoder variants with daily online fine-tuning
Averaging multiple online LGBM models followed by ensembling neural networks with varied weights/seeds/start dates
Ensembling online-updated TabM/AutoencoderMLP with offline LightGBM/XGBoost/Ridge (including k=16 ensemble within TabM)
Combining 8 models from two architectures with feature map ensembles trained on different window lengths
insights: Starting from date_id = 700 stabilizes the data without hurting performance.
Simple standardization and zero imputation are sufficient; complex preprocessing yields no gains.
Auxiliary rolling-average targets significantly improve both CV and LB scores.
Online learning via single-pass weight updates during inference is highly effective for bridging training-to-inference distribution shifts.
Inference speed optimization is critical for real-time tabular forecasting competitions.
Using GELU activations prevents dead neurons and maintains parameter utility during online learning compared to ReLU, SiLU, or PReLU.
Predicting all 9 responders simultaneously improves training stability and generalization.
Fine-tuning on a recent window of validation data significantly boosts future-day scores, establishing a foundation for the online learning strategy.
GBDTs are not always the best baseline for tabular data.
Online learning is essential for temporal financial datasets.
Cross-symbol information is required to break early score plateaus.
Adaptive time dimension treatment is necessary to surpass mid-0.90s bottlenecks.
Providing pre-computed z-scores and cross-symbol means as direct inputs improves network performance.
Training LGBM on partitions 6-9 yielded better LB results initially.
Providing cross-symbol means and z-scores as direct inputs improved performance more than letting the network compute them from raw features.
One-hot encoding time_id initially boosted LB but was later replaced by sin/cos positional encoding for better validation results.
Time-based smoothing improved performance temporarily but simpler models eventually matched it.
Treating time_id as a categorical feature significantly improves TabM performance.
R2 loss provides more robust validation stability than MSE, despite MSE yielding higher CV scores.
Online learning is highly effective for neural networks but impractical for GBDT due to computational cost and poor results.
Adding excessive lag features or auxiliary targets beyond responder_3 and responder_6 degrades performance.
Simple mean-std standardization with zero-filled NaNs works best for TabM, while filling NaNs with 3 suits AutoencoderMLP.
MSE loss yields higher CV scores but R2 loss is more robust across validation epochs, making R2 the preferred choice for stability.
Training on dates after 750 improves CV but decreases LB score, revealing a distribution shift or overfitting to recent data.
Attempting online learning on GBDT models failed and was too computationally expensive, unlike its success with neural networks.
Building a robust inference pipeline with online learning is crucial for this competition.
Neural networks (MLP, GRU, Transformer) outperformed GBDT baselines (LightGBM, XGBoost) for this dataset.
Using all responders as auxiliary targets significantly boosts both CV and LB scores.
Thorough EDA and visualization pipelines are essential for understanding fat-tailed, non-stationary time-series data.
Switching from GBDT to neural networks early was necessary because GBDT performance plateaued and negative R2 scores persisted for weeks.
Using auxiliary targets (all responders) instead of only Responder_6 greatly improved both CV and LB performance.
Online learning is crucial for financial forecasting and yields significant score gains.
Mimicking the exact submission API during training is essential to prevent data leakage and performance drops.
Financial data is non-stationary, making traditional lags ineffective as direct model inputs.
Zero-mean R² behaves distinctly from MSE around zero and is better suited as a loss function for this competition's metric.
A simple 3-layer MLP can achieve competitive results if training and architectural choices are meticulously optimized.
Focusing on generalization and careful training practices can outweigh the benefits of complex model architectures.
critical_findings: For an MLP model, the score without online learning was higher than for the GRU, but lower with online learning, indicating GRUs adapt better to incremental updates.
Performing one-day online updates for almost a year is enough to maintain strong performance, making full retraining unnecessary despite the large data gap.
Single-step online updates on the latest day of data barely improve the score, whereas fine-tuning on a multi-day window yields significant gains.
Reducing the learning rate to 1e-4 during online learning is critical for stability and performance compared to the original 1e-3.
Retraining on a longer historical window (e.g., last 56 days) less frequently (e.g., every 8 days) provides a more substantial score boost than daily updates on a shorter window (last 7 days).
Using shifted true responders as inputs in an MLM-like encoder-decoder architecture caused sky-high training metrics but zero hidden test performance.
Updating model weights on every single timestep during training was a dead end that failed to generalize to the hidden test.
Holding out the last 120 days for validation initially hurt performance but later provided necessary control over blind submissions, despite requiring full-dataset retraining.
what_did_not_work: MLP, time-series transformers, cross-symbol attention, and embeddings
Alternative NaN imputation methods
Using the entire dataset instead of filtering from date_id = 700
Single update step on the latest day of data given by the API barely helped.
XGBoost was much slower with identical results to LightGBM.
CatBoost could not match LightGBM's results.
LSTM and GRU models did not yield significant improvements.
Time-based smoothing implementation was abandoned because simpler models matched performance.
One-hot encoding of time_id yielded worse LB results than sin/cos positional encoding.
Using embedding layers for continuous features (LinearEmbeddings, PeriodicEmbeddings, PiecewiseLinearEmbeddings) did not work well.
Higher dropout (0.5) caused slower convergence, while lower dropout (0.1) led to overfitting.
Adding more auxiliary targets beyond responder_3 and responder_6 showed no obvious improvement.
Training on dates after 750 improved CV but decreased LB score.
Online learning on GBDT models did not work well and was too time-consuming.
Using GRU, LSTM, or Transformer architectures failed early in the competition.
GBDT models (LightGBM, XGBoost) could not be effectively improved.
iTransformers, patchTST, and Convnet architectures were tried but not used in the final solution.
Online training with every 120 or 200 days was considered but skipped due to pipeline complexity and the 1-minute limit.
Using shifted true responders as inputs to a decoder in an MLM-like encoder-decoder architecture.
Updating weights on each time step during training.
notable_individual_insights: rank 8 ([Private LB 8th] solution): Online learning via single-pass weight updates during inference is highly effective for bridging training-to-inference distribution shifts.
rank 17 ([Public LB 17th] Solution): Using GELU activations prevents dead neurons and maintains parameter utility during online learning compared to ReLU, SiLU, or PReLU.
rank 13 ([Public LB 13th] Our Journey to 0.0096): Cross-symbol information is required to break early score plateaus in financial time-series prediction.
rank 26 ([Public LB 26th] TabM, AutoencoderMLP with online training & GBDT offline models): R2 loss provides more robust validation stability than MSE, despite MSE yielding higher CV scores.
rank 12 ([Public LB 12th] Competition Wrap-up: Great Journey and Thank you all!): Using all responders as auxiliary targets instead of only Responder_6 greatly improved both CV and LB performance.
rank null (Few lessons learned): Mimicking the exact submission API during training is essential to prevent data leakage and performance drops.

rank null (How good can a simple solution get?): A simple 3-layer MLP can achieve competitive results if training and architectural choices are meticulously optimized, outweighing complex architectures.
